elements.sent-2411-001: In the recent article \cite{TrivIso , Farah and the authors proved a strong lifting theorem for well-behaved maps between reduced products of discrete structures, under the assumption of fairly mild Forcing Axioms.}
elements.sent-2411-002: In this note, we prove the analogue of this result in the metric setting.
elements.sent-2411-003: If $(M_n,d_n)$ is a sequence of metric spaces, we consider the equivalence relation $\sim_{\Fin}$ on $\prod_n M_n$, given by $a\sim_{\Fin b$ if and only if $\lim_n d_n(a_n,b_n)=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-004: This equivalence relation corresponds in the metric setting to `eventual equality' in the discrete setting.
elements.sent-2411-005: Moreover, in case the spaces $M_n$ have uniformly bounded diameter, quotienting by this equivalence relation gives the reduced product metric space $\prod_n M_n/\Fin$, measuring asymptotic distances between sequences.
elements.sent-2411-006: Just as metric ultraproducts, such metric reduced products are useful in several applications, for example in \cstar-algebra theory (see \cite{EffrosRosenberg or \cite{Farah.Between ).}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Reduced products can more generally serve as a tool to study approximability of certain larger objects by means of simpler finite or countable objects.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our main object of study in the present note is maps between two such reduced products $\prod_nM_n/\Fin$ and $\prod_n N_n/\Fin$, where $(N_n,\partial_n)$ is a second sequence of metric spaces.
elements.sent-2411-009: A natural and straightforward way to construct such maps is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-010: Consider a bijection $f$ between two cofinite subsets of $\bbN$ (i.e., an almost permutation of $\bbN$, which plays the role of shuffling the indices) and a sequence of maps $h_n\colon M_{f(n)}\to N_n$ for $n \in \bbN$.
elements.sent-2411-011: If the pair $(f,(h_n))$ respects asymptotic equality, meaning that whenever $a,b\in\prod_nM_n$ are such that $\lim_n d_n(a_n,b_n)=0$, we have that \begin{equation}\lim_n\partial_n(h_n(a_{f(n)}),h_n(b_{f(n)}))= 0,\end{equation} the function $\Phi[f,(h_n)]\colon\prod_nM_n\to\prod_nN_n$ defined by sending $a = (a_n)\in \prod_nM_n$ to $b= (b_n) \in \prod_n N_n$ where $b_n = h_n(a_{f(n) )$ induces a map between the corresponding reduced products over $\Fin$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Maps $\Phi\colon\prod_nM_n\to\prod_nN_n$ of this form $\Phi = \Phi[f,(h_n)]$ are called of product form, and if $\varphi\colon \prod_n M_n/\Fin\to\prod_nN_n/\Fin$ admits a lifting of product form, we say that $\varphi$ is trivial.
elements.sent-2411-013: Here $\Phi$ is called a lifting of $\varphi$ if for every $a\in \prod_nM$, the $\sim_{\Fin}$-equivalence class containing $a$ is mapped by $\phii$ to the class containing $\Phi(a)$.
elements.sent-2411-014: A key feature of trivial maps from $\prod_nM_n / \Fin$ to $\prod_nN_n / \Fin$ is that they respect a family of equivalence relations induced by pseudometrics, denoted $d^S(.\,,.\,)$ indexed by $S \in \mathcal P(\mathbb N)/\Fin$ which generalise to the metric setting the equivalence relation given by `being eventually equal on indices in $S$'.
elements.sent-2411-015: Maps preserving these equivalence relations are called coordinate respecting.
elements.sent-2411-016: In case all the structures under consideration are countable sets with the discrete metric, these notions agree with their discrete counterparts defined in \cite[\S2]{TrivIso .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The fact that reduced products of metric structures are countably saturated, together with the fact that the space of theories in a given separable language is compact, gives that, under the Continuum Hypothesis $\CH$, there exist metric spaces $M_n$ and $N_n$ and coordinate respecting functions $\prod_nM_n/\Fin\to\prod_nN_n/\Fin$ which are not trivial (see, \cite[Proposition 6]{TrivIso in case all structures are discrete, or \cite{ghasemi2014reduced for a truly metric version of this fact).}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The following, our main result, shows this is not the case under suitable Forcing Axioms.
elements.sent-2411-019: Then all coordinate respecting functions between reduced products of sequences of separable metric spaces with uniformly bounded diameter are trivial.
elements.sent-2411-020: We refer the reader to \cite{farah2022corona and \cite{TrivIso for a thorough discussion on the influence of these axioms on rigidity of massive quotient structures.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The proof of the main result stays close to that of \cite[Theorem 7]{TrivIso , with a few small technical detours needed to handle the presence of distances.}
elements.sent-2411-022: First, we prove this result for sequences of finite metric spaces: there, having fixed a coordinate respecting function $\phii$, we show that (under Forcing Axioms) there is a nonmeager ideal $\cJprod\subseteq\mathcal P(\mathbb N)$ on whose sets $A \in \cJprod$ we can construct liftings of product form (i.e., induced by a sequence of maps $h_n$ for $n\in A$).
elements.sent-2411-023: We then uniformise all such maps.
elements.sent-2411-024: Secondly, we once again uniformise (once more using crucially $\OCA$) to show our rigidity results for all reduced products of uniformly bounded sequences of separable metric spaces.
elements.sent-2411-025: Let $(M_n,d_n)$ be a sequence of separable metric spaces of uniformly bounded diameter.
elements.sent-2411-026: The metric reduced product $M:=\prod_n M_n/\Fin$ is the metric space of $\sim_{\Fin}$-equivalence classes, where, for $a=(a_n)$ and $b=(b_n)$ in $\prod_nM_n$ we write \begin{equation}a \sim_{\Fin} b  if and only if  \lim_{n} d_n(a_n,b_n)=0.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}a \sim_{\Fin} b  if and only if  \lim_{n} d_n(a_n,b_n)=0.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-027: If $a=(a_n)\in\prod_nM_n$ we write $[a]$ for its class in $M$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Every infinite $S\subseteq\mathbb N$ induces a pseudometric $d^S$ on $M$ by setting, for $a=(a_n)$ and $b=(b_n)$ in $\prod_nM_n$, \begin{equation}d^S([a],)=\limsup_{n\in S} d_n(a_n,b_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-029: If $S$ is finite, we simply set $d^S$ to be $0$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Note that $d^\mathbb N$ is precisely the metric $d_M$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Further, notice that if $S$ and $S'$ have the same image in $\mathcal P(\bbN)/\Fin$, then $d^{S}$ and $d^{S'}$ coincide (if the diameters of the factor spaces of our reduced products are uniformly bounded away from zero, this implication is an equivalence).
elements.sent-2411-032: We can thus refer to, when convenient, the pseudometric $d^S$ for $S\in\mathcal P(\bbN)/\Fin$.
elements.sent-2411-033: If $a$ and $b$ are in $M$, we write $a=_Sb$ for $d^S(a,b)=0$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Let $(M_n,d_n)$ and $(N_n,\partial_n)$ be two sequences of separable metric spaces of uniformly bounded diameter.
elements.sent-2411-036: A function $\phii\colon M\to N$ is coordinate respecting if there is $\alpha\in \Aut(\mathcal P(\bbN)/\Fin)$ such that for all $S\in\mathcal P(\bbN)/\Fin$ and all $a,b\in M$ we have that \begin{equation}a=_Sb\Rightarrow \phii(a)=_{\alpha(S)}\phii(b).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: As mentioned in the introduction, examples of these maps can be obtained starting from an almost permutation of $\mathbb N$ and applying coordinatewise maps~$h_n$.

elements.sent-2411-049: To this purpose, it is useful to consider the following sets $\cJprod^\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-050: For $\varepsilon\geq 0$, define $\cJprod^\varepsilon$ as the set of all $A\subseteq\mathbb N$ for which there exists a sequence $(h^A_n)$ of maps $h^A_n\colon M_n\to N_n$ such that for all $a\in \prod_nM_n$ we have that \begin{equation}\limsup_{n\in A}\partial_n(\Phi(a)_n,h_n(a_n))\leq\varepsilon.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-179: The following is generalising the content of \cite[\S3.1]{TrivIso .}
elements.sent-2411-180: If $Y_n$ are topological spaces, and $\cF$ is a set of partial selectors for $(Y_n)_{n\in \bbN}$, we topologize $\cF$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-181: For every $n\in \bbN,$ fix a point $0_n\in Y_n$ and embed $\cF$ in $\prod_n Y_n \times \mathcal P(\bbN)$ via the map $g \mapsto (\hat g, \dom(g))$ where $\hat g\in \prod_n Y_n$ agrees with $g$ on $\dom(g)$ and satisfies $\hat g(n)=0_n$ for $n\in \bbN\setminus \dom(g)$.
elements.sent-2411-182: Now consider the subspace topology on $\cF$ induced by this embedding.
elements.sent-2411-183: If the sets $Y_n$ are separable and metrizable spaces, then the induced topology on any family of partial selectors is again separable and metrizable.
elements.sent-2411-184: If $g$ and $h$ are partial selectors for a sequence of metric spaces $(Y_n,d_n)$, and $\varepsilon>0$, we let \begin{equation}\Diff_\varepsilon(g,h)=\{n\in \dom(g) \cap \dom(h)\mid d_n(g(n),h(n))> \varepsilon\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-185: Below, we adapt the `partial selector dichotomy' (\cite[Proposition 3.7]{TrivIso ) to the metric setting.}
elements.sent-2411-186: For distinct $x,y\in \{ 0,1\}^\mathbb{N}$, we denote by $\Delta(x,y)$ the minimal $n$ such that $x_n\neq y_n$.
elements.sent-2411-187: Let $\cF$ be a family of partial selectors for a sequence of separable metric spaces $((Y_n,d_n))_{n\in\bbN}$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-188: Then one of the following possibilities holds.
elements.sent-2411-189: There are $\cF_n$, for $n\in \bbN$, such that $\cF=\bigcup_n \cF_n$, and for all $n$ and all $g,h$ in $\cF_n$ we have $|\Diff_\varepsilon(g,h)|\leq n$.
elements.sent-2411-190: There are a perfect tree-like almost disjoint family $\cA$, an uncountable $Z\subseteq \twoN$, and an injective function $f\colon Z\to \cF$ such that for every $A\in \cA$ and all distinct $x,y$ in $Z$ we have \begin{equation}(\Diff_\varepsilon(f(x),f(y))\cap A)\setminus \Delta(x,y)\neq \emptyset.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-191: In the following we use $\OCAsharp$, a formal strenghtening of $\OCA$ (\cite[Definition 3.2]{TrivIso ).}
